widgets_values: flat print, blurry, soft edges, distorted print, pixelated, low resolution, watermark, text artifacts, smeared ink, faded white ink, transparent white, graying of whites, shirt color showing through print, uneven print, misaligned graphic, overexposed, noisy, grainy, out of focus, deformed, duplicate elements, poorly drawn, bad proportions, color bleeding, recolored print, altered design colors, washed out colors, oversaturated

photorealistic mockup, the design is printed into the fabric fibers, screen-print texture, displacement map effect, following the folds and shadows of the t-shirt, matte ink, highly detailed fabric weave

The model group loads the full Flux Fill Dev stack — UNET, dual CLIP, and VAE — which is specifically chosen for inpainting, making it ideal for generating prints within masked regions.

Anything Everywhere  Node: Rather than manually wiring MODEL, CLIP, and VAE to every downstream node, it broadcasts these signals globally across the canvas. This keeps the workflow clean and is the kind of structural decision that shows intentional workflow design rather than just connecting nodes by default.

https://github.com/Suzie1/ComfyUI_Comfyroll_CustomNodes
https://github.com/chrisgoringe/cg-use-everywhere

Thes two separate masks are the core logic of the whole workflow: One tells the model exactly where to generate the print, the other isolates the shirt itself for the custom node to recolor.

The custom node shirt color swapper, which relies on both masks being fed in correctly.

This group handles all the compositing logic that bridges the custom node output and the generation

The ImageComposite+ then places the resized print back onto the mockup using those same coordinates, and the two MaskComposite nodes (using multiply mode) combine the print and shirt masks cleanly so the inpainting model knows exactly what to touch and what to leave alone.

ShirtColorSwapper takes the mockup image and the t-shirt body mask as inputs, then recolors the shirt from its original color (white) to any target hex color.
 
The mask is what makes this precise: the color swap is contained strictly to the shirt area, leaving the background, neckline, and print zone untouched.

InpaintModelConditioning is the key node here. It combines the positive and negative prompts, the composited mockup image, and the final mask into a single conditioning package specifically formatted for Flux Fill. 

FluxGuidance sets the guidance scale at 3.5, which is the recommended range for Flux and keeps generation stable without over-sharpening.

PreviewImage gives instant visual feedback inside ComfyUI without writing to disk.

SaveImage writes the final composited mockup to disk with the filename prefix set to Shirt/Print001, meaning it saves directly into an organized folder structure.
